items.title: Polynomial degree on noisy sine data
items.scenario: You fit polynomials of degree 1, 5, and 15 to 50 noisy samples from y = sin(x). Degree 1 misses the wave (high train and val error). Degree 5 tracks the shape well. Degree 15 hits near-zero train error but wild val error.
items.explanation: Degree is the complexity knob. Too low ⇒ high bias; too high ⇒ high variance on 50 points. The sweet spot (around degree 4–6) minimizes validation error — classic U-curve in polynomial regression.

items.title: Decision tree depth for customer churn
items.scenario: A depth-1 stump on tabular churn data gets 72% accuracy on train and validation. Depth 6 reaches 94% train, 89% val. Depth 20 reaches 99% train, 84% val.
items.explanation: Shallow tree = low complexity, underfitting. Moderate depth captures interactions without memorizing every row. Very deep tree overfits — complexity past the val minimum hurts generalization.

items.title: Small image dataset with a large CNN
items.scenario: 500 labeled photos, ResNet-50 pretrained vs a 3-layer CNN. The huge model hits 98% train accuracy, 62% val. The small CNN gets 78% train, 74% val.
items.explanation: Parameter count and architecture width are complexity. On tiny data, high capacity chases noise (variance); a smaller model matches what the sample can support.

items.title: k in k-nearest neighbors for regression
items.scenario: Predict house prices with kNN. k=1 fits each training house exactly (0 train error). k=50 smooths to neighborhood averages. Validation MSE is lowest around k=8 for this city.
items.explanation: k=1 is maximum complexity (memorization); large k is low complexity (local averaging). The best k balances bias and variance for local structure vs noise.

items.title: Scaling model size after collecting 10× more data
items.scenario: A team starts with logistic regression on 2k rows (val AUC 0.78). After 20k rows, the same model plateaus at 0.80, but gradient-boosted trees at depth 8 reach 0.88 val AUC without overfitting.
items.explanation: More data shifts the viable complexity region rightward — richer models that previously overfit can now be the val-optimal choice.
